questions.question: You are descending from a cruise of 12,500 feet MSL to Death Valley Airport (-300 feet MSL), at 125 kts groundspeed and 800 feet per minute. How far out must you start down?

When filled up, your Mooney's projected weight will be 2830 pounds, 130 pounds over its max. ramp weight. Your current CG is 71.00 inches with a forward limit of 70.2 inches. Where will you remove 130 lbs from the aircraft to move the CG to the forward limit?

You are departing from Camas Municipal Airport, WA heading north to Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, WA. To check your groundspeed you use the few sections of land (1 section of land = 1 statute mile) that exist. If you cross 5 sections of land in 3 minutes and 36 seconds, how fast are you going?
If you maintain the calculated groundspeed from question 3 and a rate of climb of 700 feet per minute, will you be able to climb high enough to clear the TFR?
questions.context: Referenced question 3: You are departing from Camas Municipal Airport, WA heading north to Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, WA. To check your groundspeed you use the few sections of land (1 section of land = 1 statute mile) that exist. If you cross 5 sections of land in 3 minutes and 36 seconds, how fast are you going? [[DIVIDER]] In question 3, you were heading north towards Seattle. At 17.8 nautical miles from the center of Mt. St. Helens at 7400 feet MSL, you realize that you have forgotten about the temporary flight restriction (TFR) up to 13,000 ft MSL within a 5 nautical mile radius around the center of Mt. St. Helens.

questions.question: Tom, a former Navy pilot, one day recalls his training at Cecil Field. He remembers using an airfield to the northwest that was 5300 feet long with a 750 foot section marked off for practice carrier ops. He then wonders, what percentage of the field was not used?

One day you get the opportunity to jump into an Antonov Colt. You find the altimeter and the airspeed indicator are stuck at 630 meters and 160 km/hr, respectively. What are these readings in feet and knots?

You want to take your three nephews flying. They have all three been learning about the metric system and other weights in school. They give their weights as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] Nephew 1 = 36 kilograms [[NEXT_LINE]] Nephew 2 = 12 imperial gallons of Avgas [[NEXT_LINE]] Nephew 3 = 40.5 liters of oil [[NEXT_LINE]] What is the combined weight of your three nephews?
How far will John miss Mt. Adams by?

choices.text: He will only miss it by 1.17 nautical miles
He will miss the mountain by 11.7 nautical miles
He will miss the mountain by 7.0 nautical miles
He will only miss it by 3.10 nautical miles

questions.correctChoice: He will miss the mountain by 11.7 nautical miles
questions.context: John wants to fly directly from The Dalles, OR to Seattle, WA. However, the only problem is that there is a 12,000 foot mountain the way, Mt. Adams. There are calm winds aloft. He plans on flying the 118 nautical mile, true course of 324° route as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] 1st Fly 20 nautical miles on the original true course. [[NEXT_LINE]] 2nd Fly on a true course of 310° for 93 km. This puts you abeam Mt. Adams. [[NEXT_LINE]] 3rd Fly on a true course of 345° to regain his original course.

questions.question: Will he rejoin his course prior to getting to Seattle? If so how far from Seattle will he rejoin his course?

choices.text: No, he will need at least a correction to a true heading of 356°
Yes, he will rejoin his course 14 nautical miles from Seattle
Yes, he will rejoin his course 20 nautical miles from Seattle
Yes, he will rejoin his course 7 nautical miles from Seattle

questions.correctChoice: Yes, he will rejoin his course 14 nautical miles from Seattle
questions.context: John wants to fly directly from The Dalles, OR to Seattle, WA. However, the only problem is that there is a 12,000 foot mountain the way, Mt. Adams. There are calm winds aloft. He plans on flying the 118 nautical mile, true course of 324° route as follows: [[NEXT_LINE]] 1st Fly 20 nautical miles on the original true course. [[NEXT_LINE]] 2nd Fly on a true course of 310° for 93 km. This puts you abeam Mt. Adams. [[NEXT_LINE]] 3rd Fly on a true course of 345° to regain his original course.

questions.question: For calculation, you use a straight line distance from The Dalles to Seattle, a groundspeed of 118 km/hr, and an assumption of an extra 5.2 minutes to deviate. What is the total time enroute?

questions.question: You are 18.0 nautical miles from an airport (0 feet MSL) that is reporting a ceiling of 3300 feet. You are cruising at a groundspeed of 156 km/hr at 7500 feet MSL. You see that the cloud deck begins at 5.0 nautical miles away from the airport, in your direction. What rate of descent will you have to maintain to be 500 feet below the cloud deck before going under the clouds?
Tyra crosses the 023° radial at 13:03:42 and then crosses the 042° radial at 13:06:24. If she maintained a groundspeed of 190 statute miles per hour, how far from the VOR was she?

John travels 202 kilometers on a True Heading of 132° and finds that he is 4.3 nautical miles off course to the left. Assuming calm winds aloft, what true heading does John need to parallel his original course?
In question 13, if John has 318 km left in his flight, but he wants to regain his original course with 25 nautical miles left, what will his true heading have to be?
questions.context: Referenced question 13: John travels 202 kilometers on a True Heading of 132° and finds that he is 4.3 nautical miles off course to the left. Assuming calm winds aloft, what true heading does John need to parallel his original course?

questions.question: At 25 nautical miles from the airport, John's groundspeed is 180 km/hr at 7500 ft MSL. He wants to level at pattern altitude (1900 ft MSL) 5 nautical miles from the airport. If he wants to descend at 500 feet per minute, in how many nautical miles should he begin his descent?

choices.text: He should have started his descent 13.6 nautical miles ago

questions.question: If Tiffany and Tim are up front (40 inches), John and Genie are in back (80 inches), the fuel is onboard (70 inches), and the baggage is the back (100 inches), what is the total weight and CG?
questions.context: Tim has an aircraft with a basic empty weight of 1900 pounds (CG of 70 inches). Tim (180 pounds) has three friends he wants to take flying with him: Tiffany (120 pounds), John (270 pounds), and Genie (130 pounds). The fuel on board is 40 U.S. gallons of AVGAS and the baggage weighs 160 pounds.

questions.question: If your buddy John switches places with Tiffany, what happens to the CG?
questions.context: Tim has an aircraft with a basic empty weight of 1900 pounds (CG of 70 inches). Tim (180 pounds) has three friends he wants to take flying with him: Tiffany (120 pounds), John (270 pounds), and Genie (130 pounds). The fuel on board is 40 U.S. gallons of AVGAS and the baggage weighs 160 pounds.

questions.question: Use the information from #17 for this calculation. If you burn 30 U.S. Gallons of AVGAS on this flight, what is your CG on touchdown?

questions.question: John and Genie get out of the airplane to visit friends. Tim fills up the tanks (40 U.S. Gallons of AVGAS) and Tiffany returns to the front seat. What is the departure CG?

questions.question: One day you are flying an Airbus A380 and an FAA Inspector is onboard giving you a line check. The inspector remembers you from a previous conversation and asks if you remember the time between radials equation. You do and he asks, "If I am 49 nautical miles from a VOR and I cross the 210° radial at 14:26:34 and the 219° radial at 14:28:28, what is my groundspeed?
The Concorde is traveling at Mach 2.2, and the outside air temperature is -76°F. What is this true airspeed in statute miles per hour?
Referring to question #21, if the Concorde could maintain this for the entire 36,400 kilometer journey around the world (with calm winds everywhere), how long would this journey take?
questions.context: Referenced question 21: The Concorde is traveling at Mach 2.2, and the outside air temperature is -76°F. What is this true airspeed in statute miles per hour?

questions.question: What are the winds aloft if the true airspeed is 109 statue miles per hour, the groundspeed is 109 kilometers per hour, on a true course of 90°, and the wind correction angle is 6° L?

Maintaining a groundspeed of 140 knots, you first turn to a true heading of 213° and find that you are drifting 5° L. You then turn to a true heading of 162° and find that you are drifting 6° R. What are the winds aloft?
Without any calculations, you assume a time and distance to climb and descend for Route 1 total 25 minutes and 125 nautical miles, how long does it take to complete this route? Zero wind.
questions.context: You are flying for a corporation that has many corporate aircraft. On two separate days in two separate aircraft (a G IV and King Air), you are asked to fly from Madison, WI (800 feet MSL) to Rapid City, SD (2200 feet MSL). You fly in a standard atmosphere unless otherwise stated. Route 1 is with the G IV and route 2 is with the King Air and are as follows: Route 1 MSN to RAP direct (602 nautical miles) [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = 287° G IV burns 2000 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at Mach 0.77 (-45°C) Route 2 Leg 1 (MSN to DSM), Leg 2 (DSM to RAP) 294 nautical miles and 422 nautical miles respectively [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = Leg 1 (248°) and Leg 2 (322°) King Air burns 750 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at 230 knots, true airspeed Des Moines, IA (600 feet MSL) is a landing airport.

questions.question: Without any calculations, you assume the fuel burn in Route 2 is the average over the entire leg. You also assume, for each climb and descent, the time and distance it takes are 20 minutes and 75 nautical miles, how much fuel is burned during Route 2? Zero wind.
